title: Courting Disaster

jokes.text: Being a lawyer stinks, Geoffrey. No matter how good a job you do... your clients still compare you to Harry Hamlin.
jokes.setup: Philip complaining about his law practice
jokes.punchline: Clients compare him to the handsome TV lawyer Harry Hamlin from L.A. Law
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific joke about Philip's vanity and the 1990s pop culture landscape. Well-structured with clear setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Yes. The resemblance is quite striking.
jokes.setup: Philip's complaint about being compared to handsome Harry Hamlin
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's deadpan agreement that they look alike (they clearly don't)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey response - polite but cutting sarcasm delivered with perfect economy. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Come on, baby. Get on the soul train.
jokes.setup: Will dancing to music
jokes.punchline: His exuberant dance moves and encouraging others to join
jokes.explanation: Standard character-establishing moment showing Will's energy and street culture references.

jokes.text: That was Papa's Got a Brand New Bag... recorded 25 long years ago by James Brown... the Granddaddy of Soul. We'll be right back after this message from Geritol.
jokes.setup: DJ describing classic James Brown song
jokes.punchline: Segueing to Geritol commercial (old people medicine) after calling it an 'oldie'
jokes.explanation: Good joke about how quickly music becomes 'oldies' and targeted advertising to older demographics.

jokes.text: Will, don't play inside. You might break something. Or something might break you.
jokes.setup: Standard parental concern about breaking things
jokes.punchline: The twist that something in the fancy house might break Will instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. The reversal is unexpected and character-appropriate for the wealthy household.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. You don't have a hoop outside. And Jazz is getting tired of standing on the second-floor balcony... holding a pool skimmer.
jokes.setup: Will explaining why he plays basketball inside
jokes.punchline: The absurd makeshift basketball setup using Jazz as human hoop holder
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist visual - the image of Jazz holding a pool skimmer as a hoop is genuinely funny and original. Great character detail.

jokes.text: Come on, Prince, man. I know a playground in my neighborhood. 'Course we can only play half-court. They use the other end to sell stolen appliances.
jokes.setup: Jazz offering an alternative place to play basketball
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that half the court is used for criminal activity
jokes.explanation: Great dark humor delivered casually. Shows the contrast between worlds and Jazz's street credibility. Well-crafted setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Carlton's on the basketball team? How do they do? They try hard, but to tell you the truth, they haven't won a game so far.
jokes.setup: Will asking about Carlton's team's record
jokes.punchline: Vivian's polite way of saying they're terrible - haven't won a single game
jokes.explanation: Solid setup for Carlton's athletic inadequacy. Vivian's diplomatic phrasing is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Aunt Viv, don't you think if we went down to watch them practice... Carlton would be a little embarrassed? - Let's go. - Let's go.
jokes.setup: Will suggesting Carlton might be embarrassed by their watching
jokes.punchline: Immediate eager agreement from Jazz and Hilary to go witness the embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup with efficient payoff. The immediate enthusiasm to witness Carlton's potential humiliation is appropriately mean-spirited.

jokes.text: Man, these guys need help. Help? They need a telethon.
jokes.setup: Will saying the team needs help
jokes.punchline: Jazz escalating to suggest they need charity fundraising level help
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke with perfect economy. The telethon reference is specific and appropriately harsh.

jokes.text: He threw that ball to me. I'm on the other team. I'm supposed to try and get it. - You don't have to be rude about it. - You two, make up right now.
jokes.setup: Carlton explaining he intercepted a pass from the opposing team
jokes.punchline: His opponent being offended and the coach making them apologize for normal basketball
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy showing how overly polite and non-competitive this elite school is. Carlton's confusion is perfect.

jokes.text: Frankly, I think there's been way too much emphasis over the years... on putting the ball actually through the hoop.
jokes.setup: Coach addressing the team after terrible performance
jokes.punchline: Suggesting that scoring points is overemphasized in basketball
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist joke that perfectly captures an incompetent coach trying to rationalize failure. Highly quotable and character-defining.

jokes.text: Coach. What do you think the team's prospects are this year? For what? For winning.
jokes.setup: Alumnus asking about team prospects
jokes.punchline: Coach genuinely confused about what prospects they could have
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Coach Smiley's acceptance of failure. Great economy in the exchange.

jokes.text: Mr. Thorvald, straight up, about that fire alarm: I don't know who pulled it.
jokes.setup: Headmaster approaching Will
jokes.punchline: Will immediately confessing to something he wasn't being asked about
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's assumption that he's in trouble. Classic guilty conscience comedy.

jokes.text: Jazz, man. Of course, Mr. Jazzman.
jokes.setup: Jazz introducing himself casually
jokes.punchline: Headmaster treating 'Jazz' as a formal surname
jokes.explanation: Mild culture clash humor showing the formal headmaster misunderstanding Jazz's street nickname.

jokes.text: Man, I ain't even in this school. I'm willing to look past that if you are.
jokes.setup: Jazz pointing out he doesn't attend the school
jokes.punchline: Headmaster willing to ignore this major eligibility issue for talent
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about academic priorities vs. athletic success. Well-structured exchange.

jokes.text: All right. I'll give you my car.
jokes.setup: Coach trying various incentives to get Will to join
jokes.punchline: Final desperate offer of his personal vehicle
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing coach's desperation. Perfect economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: Your team doesn't need a playbook. They need a prayer book.
jokes.setup: Carlton offering to go through the playbook
jokes.punchline: Will's assessment that divine intervention would be more helpful than strategy
jokes.explanation: Classic wordplay joke with excellent economy. The playbook/prayer book substitution is well-executed if predictable.

jokes.text: Carlton, you got it wrong already. It's more like this. This is me. And this is you.
jokes.setup: Carlton drawing plays on blackboard
jokes.punchline: Will correcting the size ratio, presumably making himself bigger and Carlton smaller
jokes.explanation: Standard visual gag playing on height/size differences. Character-appropriate but not particularly original.

jokes.text: This is my brain... and this is your brain. This is my handsome face. And this is your ugly little Frankenstein face.
jokes.setup: Will continuing his chalk drawing comparisons
jokes.punchline: Escalating from size to intelligence to looks, with the Frankenstein insult
jokes.explanation: Predictable escalation with a generic insult. The Frankenstein reference adds some specificity but overall fairly weak.

jokes.text: Here, Ashley, finally there's an adult in the room.
jokes.setup: Childish fight between Will and Carlton
jokes.punchline: Will treating young Ashley as the mature one
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal showing that the youngest family member is acting most mature. Solid structure.

jokes.text: You've met your match, Master William.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey entering the keep-away game
jokes.punchline: His formal declaration while presumably outmaneuvering Will
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Geoffrey's formal speech even during physical comedy. Solid integration.

jokes.text: How'd you like it if I went into your room and started throwing chalk around? I'd be devastated.
jokes.setup: Philip's rhetorical question about respecting space
jokes.punchline: Will's flat, unenthusiastic response
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response that deflates Philip's attempt at parental authority. Efficient and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: All right. You were a football god. Okay. Who knows, it might do him some good. Nothing like a little organized sport to straighten a guy out. Yes. It did wonders for Pete Rose.
jokes.setup: Philip and Vivian discussing sports building character
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's sarcastic reference to Pete Rose's gambling scandal
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor referencing Pete Rose's gambling problems. Perfect Geoffrey delivery with great timing and character consistency.

jokes.text: If I didn't show up to support him, what kind of cousin would I be? It'll be three hours well spent. Three hours?
jokes.setup: Hilary expressing family loyalty to support Will
jokes.punchline: Her horror at learning the actual time commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary moment showing her superficial commitment to family. Good character consistency but predictable structure.

jokes.text: I'm going to Disneyland!
jokes.setup: Will winning his first game
jokes.punchline: Parody of Super Bowl MVP tradition
jokes.explanation: Simple pop culture reference without much twist. Character-appropriate excitement but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Don't make an assumption... because you will be an ass... and the ump will shun you.
jokes.setup: Coach correcting a late player
jokes.punchline: Torturing the 'assume' wordplay into a basketball context
jokes.explanation: Forced wordplay that doesn't quite work. Character-appropriate for the bumbling coach but poorly executed.

jokes.text: But I was late, and I have to run 10 laps. Twenty laps. Now, shut up.
jokes.setup: Carlton pointing out the unfair treatment compared to Will
jokes.punchline: Coach doubling his punishment for speaking up
jokes.explanation: Good example of unfair coach favoritism. Efficient escalation that shows the absurd double standard.

jokes.text: Okay, Banks, not paying attention? Ten laps. Not paying attention? Look at Will. He's asleep. Mind your own business, Banks. That's 20 laps.
jokes.setup: Coach punishing Banks for not paying attention
jokes.punchline: Banks pointing out Will is literally sleeping, getting punished more for speaking truth
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the favoritism theme. The absurdity of Will sleeping while others get punished for minor infractions works well.

jokes.text: Not paying attention? Look at Will. He's asleep.
jokes.setup: Banks being accused of not paying attention
jokes.punchline: Pointing out Will is literally sleeping but not getting punished
jokes.explanation: Good observation humor highlighting the absurd favoritism. The contrast is clear and funny.

jokes.text: How do you do it, Prince? Easy, Isaiah. Air Fresh gym shoes. Just pass it to Will.
jokes.setup: NBA star asking Will's secret
jokes.punchline: Will breaking fourth wall with shoe endorsement parody and honest strategy
jokes.explanation: Good meta moment with Will's characteristic self-awareness. The shoe endorsement parody fits the character's showboating nature.

jokes.text: You were great. You didn't make any baskets or anything... but you looked cute in your uniform. I think it's great how you don't get it too sweaty. I guess it's 'cause you don't run too fast. Smart move.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking Hilary about his basketball performance
jokes.punchline: Hilary's backhanded compliments that reveal how bad he actually is
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary response - vapid and accidentally cruel. Excellent character integration with her superficial perspective on athletics.

jokes.text: He did turn around to the guy behind him once and say: 'Shut up. That's my son you're talking about.'
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if Philip mentioned him during the game
jokes.punchline: Philip defending Carlton from hecklers rather than praising his play
jokes.explanation: Excellent indirect way of showing Carlton's poor performance - Philip had to defend him from critics rather than celebrate achievements.

jokes.text: Besides, if that basket were two feet lower... you'd be just as good as he is.
jokes.setup: Hilary trying to console Carlton about Will's basketball superiority
jokes.punchline: Her absurd solution involving lowering the basket
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary logic - trying to be supportive but accidentally highlighting exactly how bad Carlton is. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: True, madam, but I have found that any game can be made interesting... if you put a little money on it. $50 says Bel-Air wins it. Who'd take that bet? You rang, beautiful?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey explaining his interest in the game through betting
jokes.punchline: Jazz immediately appearing to take the seemingly impossible bet
jokes.explanation: Good setup showing Geoffrey's gambling nature and Jazz's opportunistic personality. Well-structured exchange.

jokes.text: Jazz, you bet against Will? Yeah. He screws up, I'm loaded.
jokes.setup: Hilary's shocked question about Jazz betting against his friend
jokes.punchline: Jazz's casual admission of hoping for Will's failure for financial gain
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor showing Jazz's opportunistic nature. The casual betrayal of friendship for money is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: I see a lot of new faces out here tonight... faces I didn't see when we were losing. People who did not support this team in its darkest hour. All I can say is, I don't blame you one bit. Half the time, I didn't want to be there. And quite frankly, I usually ducked out a little early.
jokes.setup: Coach addressing fair-weather fans
jokes.punchline: Admitting he also abandoned his own team during bad times
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Coach Smiley's brutal honesty and self-awareness. Great subversion of the typical coach speech.

jokes.text: Half the time, I didn't want to be there. And quite frankly, I usually ducked out a little early.
jokes.setup: Coach continuing his honest assessment of team support
jokes.punchline: Admitting he as the coach also abandoned the team early
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation. The coach admitting he didn't want to be there and left early is perfectly absurd and honest.

jokes.text: Very funny. That'll be 10 laps, chowderhead. What's the right answer? Pass it to Will.
jokes.setup: Player suggesting he should take the three-pointer
jokes.punchline: Coach's predictable response that everything should go to Will
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to the established pattern of all plays going to Will. Functional but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: Thirty, forty, fifty! There. Thanks, man. Nice doing business with you.
jokes.setup: The game ends with Will's team winning
jokes.punchline: Jazz collecting his winnings from Geoffrey
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the betting subplot. Straightforward resolution without much additional comedy.

jokes.text: Would you care to place a wager? I don't know anything about snooker. Don't worry. I'll tell you everything you need to know.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey offering another betting opportunity on snooker
jokes.punchline: Offering to educate Jazz, implying he'll manipulate the bet
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Geoffrey as a gambling hustler. The setup for future manipulation is clear.

jokes.text: Excuse me, your name is Spalding? Dad gave it to me for my birthday three years ago. It's my ball. Give it.
jokes.setup: Will sarcastically asking if Carlton's name is Spalding
jokes.punchline: Carlton misunderstanding and explaining the ball is his birthday gift
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Carlton's literal-mindedness. The basketball brand reference works for the character dynamic.

jokes.text: Boy, you're lucky that was just a starter's pistol.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Coach Smiley being upset after the game
jokes.punchline: Implying the coach was so angry he might have used a real gun
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with good economy, though the violence joke feels somewhat forced for the character.

jokes.text: I been here for two months. I been getting yelled at at home... screamed at at school, I been sent to detention, grounded... threatened with expulsion on almost a daily basis. I finally do something I get credit for, and you can't take it.
jokes.setup: Will defending his ball-hogging behavior
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of his troubles at the fancy school
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's frustration with his new environment. The escalating list of punishments is well-structured.

jokes.text: No, I came up with all that fly stuff myself.
jokes.setup: Philip listing Will's showboat moves during the game
jokes.punchline: Will proudly taking credit for the excessive showboating
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Will's pride in his style over substance. Efficient delivery.

jokes.text: Did I mention I was elected editor of the yearbook?
jokes.setup: Philip explaining he attends all of Carlton's activities
jokes.punchline: Carlton immediately adding another activity to the overwhelming list
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton moment showing his overachieving nature. Great timing after Philip's complaint about keeping up with his activities.

tmdb_overview: Carlton feels overshadowed by Will, who's become a basketball star at school.
